docs.title: repo-guide
docs.text:
The repo-guide repository is a Python project that uses AI to generate documentation for code repositories. It uses the click library to create a command-line interface (CLI) that allows users to specify the repository to document, as well as various options such as the LLM model to use, a token budget, and custom instructions for the LLM. It outputs Markdown documentation files and a configuration file for MkDocs which can be used to generate a static HTML website. The repository uses the following dependencies:

  • bleach-allowlist and bleach: Used for sanitizing the generated HTML, ensuring only Markdown-compatible HTML is rendered.
  • click: Used for creating the command-line interface.
  • gitpython: Used for interacting with Git repositories, determining the repository's URL, and filtering files based on .gitignore.
  • llm-gemini and llm: Used for interacting with language models. llm is the base library for interacting with language models, and llm-gemini provides the Gemini-specific integration.
  • mkdocs-material and mkdocs: Used for generating a static documentation site from the generated Markdown files.
  • tqdm: Used for displaying progress bars during the documentation generation process.
  • tiktoken: Used for counting tokens for OpenAI models.
  • magika (optional): Used for filtering out non-text files.

The core functionality is implemented in the src/repo_guide package. The CLI is defined in src/repo_guide/cli.py, and the core logic for generating the documentation is implemented in the DocGenerator class, also in src/repo_guide/cli.py.

The repository is tested using pytest, with the tests defined in tests/test_repo_guide.py.

The repository also uses GitHub Actions for continuous integration and deployment. The workflows are defined in the .github/workflows directory:

  • test.yml runs the tests on every push and pull request.
  • publish_site.yml builds and deploys the documentation to GitHub Pages after each release.
  • publish.yml publishes the package to PyPI after each release.

The root directory also includes important configuration files:

  • README.md provides a high-level overview of the project, installation instructions, usage examples, and troubleshooting tips. It also links to the project's generated documentation, which serves as a good example of what repo-guide does.
  • .pre-commit-config.yaml configures the pre-commit hooks that automatically run ruff for linting and formatting.
  • .gitignore specifies the files that Git should ignore.
  • .gitattributes configures how Git handles line endings.
  • .python-version specifies the Python version used in the project.
  • pyproject.toml contains project metadata, dependencies, and build configurations.
  • LICENSE contains the Apache 2.0 license for the project.

The repo-guide tool takes a path to a repository (or a subdirectory of a repository) as input. It then walks through the directories, and for each directory it uses the DocGenerator class to build an XML prompt. The prompt includes the path to the directory, paths to its subdirectories, and the paths and contents of files in the directory (truncated if necessary). It also includes any existing README.md files in the directory, allowing the LLM to augment and edit the documentation. The tool then uses an LLM to generate a README.md file describing the directory. Finally, the tool generates an mkdocs.yml file and an index.md file that allows a user to browse the documentation with mkdocs serve.

docs.title: .github
docs.text:
The .github directory contains configuration and resources related to GitHub's features, such as Actions workflows.

The workflows subdirectory contains the GitHub Actions workflow definitions for the repository. These workflows automate various tasks such as testing, documentation deployment, and package publishing.

  • workflows/test.yml: This workflow defines the automated tests run on each push and pull request. It sets up Python using uv, installs the project, and runs pytest. The workflow is run on Ubuntu with Python versions 3.11 and 3.12.

  • workflows/publish_site.yml: This workflow is responsible for publishing the self-documentation generated by repo-guide. It triggers on the creation of a new release or manually by dispatch. It first runs the tests defined in test.yml. If the tests pass, it then builds the documentation using repo-guide and deploys it to GitHub Pages using mkdocs gh-deploy. The workflow utilizes a cache for mkdocs-material to speed up subsequent runs and uses the LLM_GEMINI_KEY to access the Gemini model if it is set.

  • workflows/publish.yml: This workflow automates the publishing of the Python package to PyPI when a new release is created. It first runs the tests from test.yml. If the tests pass, it builds the package and publishes it to PyPI using the pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish action. The environment: release and id-token: write permissions are required to publish to PyPI.

These workflows work together to ensure the quality of the code, provide documentation for users, and make the package available to the community. The test.yml provides a basic level of testing that the other two workflows depend on. The publish_site.yml workflow uses repo-guide to generate the documentation for the project, and publish.yml uses uv to build the package for distribution.

docs.title: workflows
docs.text:
This directory contains the GitHub Actions workflow definitions for the repository. These workflows automate various tasks like testing, documentation deployment, and package publishing.

  • test.yml: This workflow defines the automated tests run on each push and pull request. It sets up Python using uv, installs the project, and runs pytest. The workflow is run on Ubuntu with Python versions 3.11 and 3.12.

  • publish_site.yml: This workflow is responsible for publishing the self-documentation generated by repo-guide. It triggers on the creation of a new release or manually by dispatch. It first runs the tests defined in test.yml. If the tests pass, it then builds the documentation using repo-guide and deploys it to GitHub Pages using mkdocs gh-deploy. The workflow utilizes a cache for mkdocs-material to speed up subsequent runs and uses the LLM_GEMINI_KEY to access the Gemini model if it is set.

  • publish.yml: This workflow automates the publishing of the Python package to PyPI when a new release is created. It first runs the tests from test.yml. If the tests pass, it builds the package and publishes it to PyPI using the pypa/gh-action-pypi-publish action. The environment: release and id-token: write permissions are required to publish to PyPI.

These workflows work together to ensure the quality of the code, provide documentation for users, and make the package available to the community. The test.yml provides a basic level of testing that the other two workflows depend on. The publish_site.yml workflow uses repo-guide to generate the documentation for the project, and publish.yml uses uv to build the package for distribution.

docs.title: src
docs.text:
The repo-guide/src directory contains the source code for the repo-guide tool.

The core logic is located in the repo_guide subdirectory, which is a Python package. The key files are:

  • __main__.py: This is the entry point for the command-line interface (CLI), calling the cli function from cli.py.
  • cli.py: This file implements the CLI using the click library and the DocGenerator class, handling command-line arguments, initializing the DocGenerator, walking the input directory, prompting the language model, writing the generated documentation, and configuring a local documentation server.
  • DocGenerator class: Located within cli.py, this class is responsible for orchestrating the documentation generation:
    • The __init__ method initializes the DocGenerator and sets up the repository object and language model.
    • The _prompt_with_backoff method handles requests to the LLM with exponential backoff.
    • The _safe_read_file method safely reads file contents with different encodings.
    • The _forward_slash_path method ensures paths use forward slashes.
    • The _build_prompt method constructs the XML input for the LLM, including directory paths, subdirectories, file content (truncated if necessary), and existing README.md files.
    • The _build_system_prompt method constructs the system prompt to guide the LLM.
    • The load_existing_docs method loads existing documentation if resuming is enabled.
    • The generate_docs method walks through the input directory, filters files, and calls the LLM to generate documentation which is saved as README.md files in the output directory.
    • The write_mkdocs_configuration writes the mkdocs.yml file for use by MkDocs, with an on_page_content hook to clean the generated HTML.

These files work together to provide the core functionality of repo-guide. The cli.py module serves as the interface between the user and the underlying DocGenerator class. The __main__.py file enables the execution of the cli.py module from the command line.

docs.title: repo_guide
docs.text:
The repo_guide/src/repo_guide directory contains the core logic of the repo-guide tool. It is a Python package, indicated by the presence of an __init__.py file.

The __main__.py file is the entry point for the command-line interface (CLI). It imports the cli function from cli.py and executes it when the script is run directly. This makes the package executable from the command line.

The cli.py file is the heart of the application, implementing the DocGenerator class which generates the documentation and the CLI using the click library. The cli function defines the command-line interface for repo-guide, including options for input and output directories, LLM model selection, token counting, and more. This file is responsible for:

  • Parsing command-line arguments.
  • Initializing the DocGenerator class.
  • Walking the input directory.
  • Prompting the language model.
  • Writing the generated documentation to the output directory.
  • Writing an mkdocs.yml configuration file.
  • Optionally starting a local documentation server.
  • Filtering files and directories using ignore patterns, git metadata, or magika if available.

The DocGenerator class does the bulk of the work, orchestrating the documentation generation process:

  • The __init__ method initializes the DocGenerator class, sets up the repository object, resolves paths, and configures the language model. It also attempts to determine the repository's URL and file prefix for creating links in the generated documentation.
  • The _prompt_with_backoff method handles making requests to the LLM with exponential backoff in case of errors.
  • The _safe_read_file method safely reads file contents, handling different encodings.
  • The _forward_slash_path method ensures path separators are forward slashes, as required by the XML.
  • The _build_prompt method constructs the XML input for the LLM, including the current directory's path, subdirectories, and files. It also includes the content of existing README.md files to provide context. It truncates file contents if the --files-token-limit is set.
  • The _build_system_prompt method constructs the system prompt that instructs the LLM how to process the XML input. It includes custom instructions if provided.
  • The load_existing_docs method loads existing documentation from the filesystem if resuming is enabled.
  • The generate_docs method walks through the input directory, filtering out unwanted files and directories. For each directory, it calls the LLM with a generated prompt, and then saves the resulting output as a README.md file in the output directory.
  • The write_mkdocs_configuration writes the mkdocs.yml file for use by MkDocs. The on_page_content hook cleans the generated HTML by stripping any non-markdown tags and attributes.

Together, these files provide the core functionality of repo-guide. The cli.py module is the interface between the user and the underlying logic of the DocGenerator class. The __main__.py file makes the cli.py module executable from the command line.

docs.title: tests
docs.text:
The repo-guide/tests directory contains the tests for the repo-guide project. The most important file is test_repo_guide.py, which contains all of the tests for the project.

The tests use the pytest framework and the click library for testing the command-line interface.

Several tests are focused on the prompt construction for the language model. These tests create a mock repository using git, complete with a remote origin and a few files. Then the tests create a DocGenerator object, and use it to create a prompt that is in the form of an XML document. This XML is designed to provide information about the repository to the language model in a structured format. The XML includes the <current_directory>, <subdirectories>, and <files> tags. The tests then assert that the prompt is constructed correctly.

Specifically:

  • test_prompt_construction tests that the prompt generated for the root of the test repository includes the README.md file and the src and src/utils subdirectories with their corresponding generated readmes.
  • test_subdirectory_prompt_construction tests that the prompt generated for the src subdirectory includes main.py and the utils subdirectory, using the generated readme for the utils directory.

The test_file_decoding_failures test ensures that the code can handle files that can't be decoded, such as binary files, or text files with encodings that are not UTF-8, latin-1 or CP1252. Files that can't be decoded are not included in the prompt.

The test_repo_url and test_repo_url_with_subdirectory tests check that the repo_url and repo_url_file_prefix properties of the DocGenerator class are correctly constructed based on the input directory, which can be the root of the repository or a subdirectory. This is important because the code will use these URLs to create links to the files in the generated documentation.

Finally, the test_doc_generation_end_to_end and test_doc_generation_src_directory tests use the click testing framework to invoke the command-line interface and check that the documentation is generated correctly. This tests the entire pipeline from parsing the command line arguments to generating the documentation files. They use a mocked LLM that returns a truncated version of the input prompt so that tests don't require a real LLM to run. The test_doc_generation_end_to_end test runs the tool on the root of the test repository, and the test_doc_generation_src_directory test runs it on the src directory of the test repository.
